blockquote.p: Note to Balloters: The key changes made since FHIR 6.0.0-ballot4 include:
Changed the Composition.participant.type short description to "Meaning and purpose of participation, in creation of the clinical document" as a correction.
Added Composition.classification BackboneElement to provide classification data.
Updated the descriptions and constraint for the Composition.name element and added an invariant test.
Changed the binding for Composition.attester.mode to be the THO ValueSet and the example now uses the THO CodeSystem.
Added an example for Context-Establishing Clinical Snapshot.
Removed the "Note to Implementers" section and removed a note about document signatures from the "Operation $document on Composition" page.
Updated the Composition resource description and definition (both the same text).
Improved the "Scope and Usage" section text.
In the "Boundaries and Relationships" section a bullet for DiagnosticReport was added and clarified the List boundary on the right axis.
Clarified text, added a paragraph, and the meaning of "subject" in the "Compositions about multiple entities" section.
Fixes for the Search on section.code and section.text.div expressions
Removed a sentence from the comment for Composition.title about the cardinality of the element in an older version.
Added CareTeam and Group as possible resource types for Composition.participant.party Reference.
CatalogType CodeSystem/ValueSet ownership moved to Orders and Observations workgroup

div.p: ISO/TR 12773-2:2009 defines the term EHR composition as a "set of information committed to one EHR by one agent, as a result of a single clinical encounter or record documentation" and offers these examples: Progress Note, radiology report, referral letter, clinic visit record, discharge summary, functional health assessment, diabetes review. FHIR adopts this concept as the Composition resource — the structural unit that binds a committed set of information to a subject, authors, and context, along with the attested narrative that represents the information. Where ISO frames an EHR composition as the product of one encounter or documentation event, FHIR carries the same structure into two use cases that differ in how they are packaged and how long they persist:

ul.li: FHIR Documents — attestable, immutable Bundles with composed narrative.
Context-Establishing Snapshots — an annotated, attested list of queryable FHIR resources that represents the current status of the subject.

div.p: A Composition is the basic structure from which FHIR Documents — immutable bundles with attested narrative — are built. Composition defines the structure and narrative content necessary for a document. However, a Composition alone does not constitute a document. Rather, the Composition is the first entry in a Bundle where Bundle.type=document, and any other resources referenced from Composition must be included as subsequent entries in the Bundle (for example, Patient, Practitioner, Encounter, etc.). A single logical composition may be associated with a series of derived documents, each of which is a frozen copy of the composition. See the FHIR Documents section for more information on the document structure, presentation, and processing.
The Clinical Document profile constrains Composition to specify a clinical document (matching CDA). See also the comparison with CDA.

The Composition can be used to record the current status of the subject. This use of Composition supports persistence and clinical attestation beyond simple enumeration. The sections establish the relationship of the entries within the overall context and provide information about the relevant parties involved in assembling the information. Unlike FHIR Documents, this use of Composition serves as the structural backbone for non-document artifacts and changes as the subject's status changes.

div.h3: Relationship of Composition Elements to Provenance
div.p: Some Composition elements overlap with information that may also be represented using Provenance. Composition elements describe information intrinsic to the Composition and SHOULD be used in preference to Provenance. Provenance SHOULD be used when additional information about the activity that created, revised, transformed, removed, or signed the Composition is required. When both are present, the information SHOULD be consistent.

ul.li: Amendment or correction: Composition.status describes the clinical standing of the current Composition, while Composition.relatesTo identifies the previous artifact—for example, status = corrected with relatesTo.type = corrects. Provenance may additionally identify the revision activity, prior entity, responsible agents, time, and reason.

Derivation or transformation: relatesTo.type = derived-from or transforms states the relationship between the artifacts. Provenance may describe the activity and the source entities used to produce the Composition.

Retraction: relatesTo.type = retracts states that the Composition retracts another artifact. This is distinct from Provenance.entity.role = removal, which describes how an entity was used in an activity involving its removal.

Attestation and signature: Composition.attester identifies responsibility for the accuracy of the Composition, while Provenance.signature records a signature on the target. relatesTo.type = signs is used only when the Composition itself is a signature artifact for the target, not merely to indicate that the Composition was signed.

div.p: Composition is a structure for grouping information for purposes of persistence and attestability. The Composition resource defines a set of healthcare-related information that is assembled together into a single logical document that provides a single coherent statement of meaning, establishes its own context and that has clinical attestation with regard to who is making the statement. The Composition resource provides the basic structure of a FHIR document. The full content of the document is expressed using a Bundle containing the Composition and its entries.
There are several other grouping structures in FHIR with distinct purposes:

ul.li: The List resource — provides a flat enumeration of resources with per-entry flags (added, removed, deleted) and an optional snapshot/working mode. Composition differs from List in providing annotation and section structure: titled and coded sections, per-section narrative, per-section authorship, and document-level attestation. Use List for flat collections (dynamic or snapshot) where per-entry tracking is the primary need; use Composition for annotated, structured collections (typically packaged as a FHIR document) where sections, narrative, and attestation matter.

The Group resource — defines a group of specific people, animals, devices, etc. by enumerating them, or by describing qualities that group members have. The Group resource refers to other resources, possibly implicitly. Groups are intended to be acted upon or observed as a whole (e.g., performing therapy on a group, calculating risk for a group, etc.). This resource will commonly be used for public health (e.g., describing an at-risk population), clinical trials (e.g., defining a test subject pool) and similar purposes.

The Bundle resource — is an infrastructure container for a group of resources. It does not have narrative and is used to group collections of resources for transmission, persistence or processing (e.g., messages, documents, transactions, query responses, etc.). The content of bundles is typically algorithmically determined for a particular exchange or persistence purpose.

A DiagnosticReport resource — is used strictly to compile a collection of diagnostic information and focuses on the discrete data in the report, but may contain or reference a separate rendered version of the report. If you have a highly structured report, use DiagnosticReport. For narrative-driven reports, the Composition resource would be more appropriate.

The QuestionnaireResponse resource — is similar to Composition in that both organize collections of items and can have a hierarchical structure. Questionnaires are also intended to help guide 'human' presentation of data. However, Compositions organize resources, while Questionnaires/QuestionnaireResponses organize specific elements. Also, a Questionnaire represents data 'to be gathered' and is subject-independent, while Compositions represent collections of data that are complete and are about a particular subject. It is possible for StructureDefinitions to act as 'templates' for FHIR documents that guide what data is collected for a particular purpose (e.g. a referral), but this differs from the gathering process that a Questionnaire provides where there are specific questions that must be asked and answered and rules that guide which questions are enabled in which circumstances.

div.p: The Composition resource organizes clinical and administrative content into sections, each of which contains a narrative, and references other resources for supporting data. The narrative content of the various sections in a Composition are supported by the resources referenced in the section entries. The complete set of content to make up a document includes the Composition resource together with various resources pointed to or indirectly connected to the Composition. See the FHIR Documents documentation for guidance on how a Composition is used when creating a document bundle.

div.div:
Background and Context
Composition Status Codes
Every composition has a status element, which describes the status of the content of the composition, taken from this list of codes:
<%codelist http://hl7.org/fhir/composition-status%>
Composition status generally only moves down through this list — it moves from registered or preliminary to final and then it may progress to amended. Note that in many workflows, only final compositions are made available and the preliminary status is not used.
A very few compositions are created entirely in error in the workflow — usually the composition concerns the wrong patient or is written by the wrong author, and the error is only detected after the composition has been used or documents have been derived from it. To support resolution of this case, the composition is updated to be marked as entered-in-error and a new derived document can be created. This means that the entire series of derived documents is now considered to be created in error and systems receiving derived documents based on retracted compositions SHOULD remove data taken from earlier documents from routine use and/or take other appropriate actions. Systems are not required to provide this workflow or support documents derived from retracted compositions, but they SHALL NOT ignore a status of entered-in-error. Note that systems that handle compositions or derived documents and don't support the error status need to define some other way of handling compositions that are created in error; while this is not a common occurrence, some clinical systems have no provision for removing erroneous information from a patient's record, and there is no way for a user to know that it is not fit for use - this is not safe.
Note for CDA aware readers
Many users of this specification are familiar with the Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) and related specifications. CDA is a primary design input to the Composition resource (other principal inputs are other HL7 document specifications and EN13606). There are three important structural differences between CDA and the Composition resource:
A Composition is a logical construct. Composition and the resources referenced in its sections are wrapped into a document Bundle for exchange of the whole package (the composition and its parts). In FHIR a CDA document is represented as a document Bundle. The CDA document identifiers map as:
ClinicalDocument.id ↔ Bundle.identifier,
ClinicalDocument.setId ↔ Composition.identifier,
and ClinicalDocument.versionNumber ↔ Composition.version.
In addition, ClinicalDocument.confidentialityCode is equivalent to Bundle.meta.security. For other CDA mappings to Composition, see the mappings page.
The composition section defines a section (or sub-section) of the document, but unlike CDA, the section entries are actually references to other resources that hold the supporting data content for the section. This design means that the data can be reused in many other ways.
Unlike CDA, the context defined in the Composition (the subject, author, event, event period and encounter) apply to the composition and do not specifically apply to the resources referenced from the section.entry. There is no context flow model in FHIR, so each resource referenced from within a Composition expresses its own individual context. In this way, clinical content can safely be extracted from the composition.
In addition, note that both the code lists (e.g., Composition.status) and the Composition resource are mapped to HL7 v3 and/or CDA.
